common_name: Top Hat Blueberry (Dwarf Container Blueberry, Patio Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5) well-drained soil or in large containers (15+ gallons) filled with peat-based acidic mix; Top Hat is a true dwarf blueberry (18–24 inches tall) that grows well in containers on patios, decks, and balconies — the only named blueberry variety that is genuinely practical for container culture in small spaces without requiring large raised beds; plant two for cross-pollination since Top Hat is not reliably self-fruitful.

directions: Plant in highly acidic, well-drained soil (pH 4.5–5.5) or containers filled with peat-based mix; fertilize with acid-forming fertilizer (ammonium sulfate or azalea fertilizer) in early spring; Top Hat requires two plants for best pollination and fruit set; berries ripen mid-season (July in zones 5–6) — medium-sized, sweet, and flavorful; does not need pruning for first 4–5 years. Top Hat is the most widely grown dwarf container blueberry and the standard recommendation from extension services for gardeners wanting to grow blueberries in containers, small raised beds, or on patios where the 4–6 foot size of standard highbush varieties is impractical. The variety is a University of Michigan-developed dwarf highbush × lowbush half-high cross that was specifically selected for its unusually compact habit — plants grow only 18–24 inches tall and wide, far smaller than either highbush (4–6 feet) or lowbush (6–12 inches, spreads widely). This intermediate compact size makes Top Hat ideal for large container culture in a way that no other named blueberry variety achieves: large enough to be productive, small enough to manage easily in a 15–20 gallon container, and cold-hardy enough to survive winters outdoors in zones 4–7 on a patio or protected corner. The berries are medium-sized, deep blue, and well-flavored for a half-high type — sweeter and more aromatic than wild lowbush berries and with good quality comparable to standard highbush varieties. The plants are also attractive ornamentally: brilliant red fall foliage makes Top Hat an attractive container plant throughout the growing season. One of the most important blueberry introductions specifically designed for urban and suburban small-space gardening.
sources: University of Michigan — Blueberry Research Program
Michigan State University Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Selection

common_name: Jersey Blueberry (Classic Northern Highbush Blueberry, New Jersey Standard Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.2), well-drained, humus-rich soil in full sun; Jersey requires cross-pollination — plant with a compatible highbush variety such as Bluecrop, Duke, or Blueray for reliable fruit set and heavier yields; Jersey is a late-season highbush variety ripening in August in most of the Northeast, extending the fresh blueberry season after early and mid-season varieties have finished.

directions: Plant in very acidic, well-drained soil amended with peat moss or pine bark; maintain soil pH at 4.5–5.2 for healthy growth and production; mulch heavily with pine bark or acid wood chips; fertilize with ammonium sulfate in early spring; Jersey ripens in August (late season) producing large, firm, light blue berries with a mild, sweet flavor and excellent storage quality. Jersey blueberry is one of the oldest and most historically important highbush blueberry varieties in American commercial and home garden production — developed by Frederick Coville at the USDA Beltsville Agricultural Research Center in 1928 from deliberate crosses with wild Vaccinium corymbosum selections from the Atlantic coastal plain. Jersey was one of the first named highbush blueberry varieties released and became the cornerstone of the New Jersey commercial blueberry industry (the first commercial blueberry industry in the United States), for which it was specifically developed. For several decades, Jersey was essentially THE commercial highbush blueberry of the American East Coast, dominating production in New Jersey, Michigan, and the other blueberry-producing states before the development of newer varieties with earlier ripening or larger fruit. Despite its age (nearly 100 years of commercial production), Jersey remains popular for several reasons: its late-season ripening (August) extends the fresh blueberry window after most other varieties have finished, its very large, firm, light blue berries store and ship exceptionally well (better than softer-berried varieties), and its mild, sweet flavor is reliable and consistent. Jersey is considered somewhat milder in flavor than Bluecrop or Elliott but produces reliably in a wide range of conditions.
sources: USDA Beltsville Agricultural Research Center — Jersey blueberry release
Rutgers Cooperative Extension — Highbush Blueberry Production
Michigan State University Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Selection

common_name: Northblue Blueberry (Half-High Cold-Hardy Highbush Blueberry, Northern Half-High)

planting.note: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.2), well-drained, organic-rich soil in full sun; Northblue is a University of Minnesota-developed half-high blueberry variety with exceptional cold-hardiness (to -30°F or colder when established and mulched) specifically for zones 3–5 where standard highbush varieties suffer severe winter dieback; plant with Northcountry, Northsky, or Polaris for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in very acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.2) with excellent drainage; mulch with pine bark 3–4 inches deep to protect roots and maintain moisture; fertilize with acidifying fertilizer (ammonium sulfate) in spring; Northblue ripens mid-season (July in Minnesota and Wisconsin) producing large, dark blue berries with excellent highbush flavor — larger and better-flavored than lowbush but hardier than standard highbush. Northblue blueberry is one of the University of Minnesota-developed half-high blueberry varieties that transformed blueberry growing in zones 3–5 of the upper Midwest, making reliable blueberry production possible in climates where standard highbush varieties (which are typically hardy only to about -20°F with snow cover) suffered severe dieback or winter-killed entirely. The half-high blueberries were developed by crossing standard highbush Vaccinium corymbosum (for berry size and quality) with the ultra-cold-hardy native lowbush blueberry Vaccinium angustifolium (for hardiness) — and Northblue represents one of the earliest and most successful results of this breeding work. Northblue plants grow 2–3 feet tall — intermediate between lowbush (6–18 inches) and highbush (4–6 feet) — and produce large, dark blue berries with a distinctly better flavor and larger size than wild lowbush berries, closer to highbush quality. The combination of cold-hardiness to -35°F (with some snow cover and mulching), compact size, and genuinely good fruit quality (not just acceptable — actually good by highbush standards) makes Northblue one of the most important blueberry varieties for cold-climate gardeners. Cross-pollination with another half-high variety (Northcountry, Northsky, Polaris) is required for best fruit set.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Evaluation
University of Wisconsin-Extension — Small Fruit Variety Trials

common_name: Northcountry Blueberry (Half-High Minnesota Cold-Hardy Blueberry, Twin Cities Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.2), well-drained, organic-rich soil in full sun; Northcountry is a University of Minnesota half-high blueberry with extreme cold-hardiness (to -30°F) designed for zones 3–5; plant with Northblue, Northsky, or Polaris for cross-pollination; the compact plants (2–3 feet) are particularly suited to small gardens and landscaping in cold climates.

directions: Plant in very acidic, well-drained soil (pH 4.5–5.2); mulch heavily; fertilize lightly with ammonium sulfate; Northcountry ripens slightly earlier than Northblue (late June to early July in Minnesota), making it valuable for extending the blueberry season from the beginning; berries are medium-sized, sky-blue, with a mild but pleasant flavor and good sweetness; the plants have attractive red-orange fall color. Northcountry blueberry is a companion half-high variety to Northblue from the same University of Minnesota breeding program, released together as a pair that complements Northblue's mid-season ripening with an earlier season (typically ripening 1–2 weeks before Northblue). The pairing of Northcountry (early) with Northblue (mid) or Northsky (late) is the standard recommendation from University of Minnesota Extension for home blueberry plantings in zones 3–5, providing a 3–4 week fresh blueberry season from one combined planting. The cross-pollination between these varieties also improves fruit set on both plants. Northcountry plants grow slightly more compact than Northblue (typically 24–36 inches rather than 36–48 inches), with a dense, mounded habit that makes them particularly attractive as landscape shrubs in addition to their edible fruit value. The berries are medium-sized (slightly smaller than Northblue), sky-blue in color, with a good sweet-mild flavor and good shelf life for a half-high variety. The extreme cold-hardiness — tested to -30°F with snow cover — and early ripening season make Northcountry one of the most important blueberry varieties for northern Minnesota, North Dakota, and other severe cold-climate gardening situations.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Evaluation
University of Wisconsin-Extension — Small Fruit Variety Trials
North Dakota State University Extension — Fruit Production

common_name: Aurora Blueberry (Very Late Season Northern Highbush Blueberry, Season-Extender Blueberry)

planting.note: Plant in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.2), well-drained, organic-rich soil in full sun; Aurora is a Michigan State University-developed very late season northern highbush blueberry — ripening in late August to mid-September in Michigan and the Northeast, 4–6 weeks after early season varieties like Duke — the latest-ripening major highbush blueberry variety, allowing fresh blueberry harvest to extend well into September; plant with an early or mid-season variety for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in very acidic, well-drained, humus-rich soil; maintain pH 4.5–5.2 with acid fertilizer; mulch with pine bark; Aurora ripens very late (late August through September) producing large, firm, light blue berries with outstanding flavor — considered by MSU researchers to have among the best flavor of any northern highbush variety; plant early-season varieties (Duke, Bluecrop) alongside for cross-pollination and a full-season harvest. Aurora blueberry is a Michigan State University-released variety (2003) that has become highly regarded among serious home blueberry growers and specialty producers for its combination of very late ripening season and exceptional berry quality. The variety was specifically developed as part of MSU's late-season breeding program aimed at extending the Michigan commercial blueberry season into September, reducing market glut during the peak July–August production and commanding higher prices for September-ripening fruit. Aurora ripens the latest of any major highbush blueberry variety — typically 4–6 weeks after early-season varieties like Duke and 2–3 weeks after Jersey (itself a late variety), making September fresh blueberries possible in zones 4–6 where frost would otherwise end the season. The berries are large to very large, firm (excellent handling and shelf life for a highbush variety), and have a notably good flavor — sweet, aromatic, and complex — that is consistently rated among the best of all highbush varieties in blind flavor tastings. The very firm texture also makes Aurora berries excellent for fresh eating (they snap satisfyingly rather than bursting), baking, and freezing. An excellent choice for home orchardists wanting to extend fresh blueberry season as late as possible into autumn.
sources: Michigan State University Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Selection
Rutgers Cooperative Extension — Highbush Blueberry Production
